Article.ArticleTitle: A multi-centric study on the organization of infection prevention and control programmes in tertiary-care public-sector hospitals of India: perspectives from a low- and middle-income country.

Abstract.AbstractText: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) are a major public health concern in low- and middle-income countries, related to the inconsistent implementation of infection prevention and control (IPC) measures, thereby increasing the risk of antimicrobial resistance and patient harm. This study aimed to observe the organization and implementation of IPC programmes among the tertiary care hospitals of India.

All India Institute of Medical Sciences conducted a multi-centric, observational-cross-sectional study involving infection control professionals in 31 healthcare institutions across 15 states and Union Territories of India. A validated questionnaire covering six IPC domain programmes, guidelines, education, surveillance, monitoring and infrastructure was administered during a training of trainers programme for IPC for pandemic preparedness.

Most of the surveyed institutions (97%) were public sector hospitals with the majority (60%) having fewer than 500 beds. IPC committees showed clinical representation with only 58.1% including epidemiologists. While HAI training was provided in 74.5% facilities and 61.4% had antibiotic policies, only 27.5% benchmarked HAI data. Surveillance and emergency preparedness varied, with 53.3% reporting isolation rooms and 48.9% having surge plans. Standardized operating procedures for IPC are available, however, improvement is needed for biological threat preparedness.

IPC frameworks are strengthening in public sector hospitals but opportunities for improvement exist in the surveillance, benchmarking, human resources and surge preparedness during outbreaks. Focused improvements and policy measures are needed at the hospital level to bridge these gaps to ensure system-wide IPC effectiveness for pandemic preparedness.
